steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank is the subject of the sentence: a title ("German biologist"), a name ("Gertrud Morlock"), and "colleagues" joined by "and." The question tests whether commas belong between these elements. The rule: a restrictive appositive — one that identifies exactly who the noun refers to — is not set off by commas.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — German biologist, Gertrud Morlock, and colleagues,: Puts commas around "Gertrud Morlock," treating it as a nonessential appositive, and adds a trailing comma after "colleagues" that severs the subject from the verb. Both errors. ✗
  • Choice B — German biologist Gertrud Morlock, and colleagues: Correctly drops the comma between "German biologist" and "Gertrud Morlock," but inserts an incorrect serial comma before "and" in a two-item list ("Morlock and colleagues"). Only two items are joined, so no comma before "and" is needed. ✗
  • Choice C — German biologist, Gertrud Morlock, and colleagues: Same as A but without the trailing comma — still incorrectly sets "Gertrud Morlock" off with commas as if nonessential. ✗
  • Choice D — German biologist Gertrud Morlock and colleagues: No comma between the title and the name (restrictive appositive), and no comma before "and" in a two-item compound. Clean, correct subject. ✓
That matches Choice D — German biologist Gertrud Morlock and colleagues.
